title: A Guided Look at the Biblion Website Prototype
excerpt: A preview-style post that walks through the current website shell and explains why it matters for the public-facing story.
call_to_action: If this kind of build-note is useful, future deep-dive previews will move back into the members-only stream.

links.label: Website entry document
links.note: This is the HTML entry point that mounts the React app driven by App.jsx.

links.label: React application shell
links.note: This component drives the graph-based prototype experience behind the entry document.

sections.heading: Why this post exists
paragraphs: This is the sort of development note I would normally reserve for members, because it sits close to the working edge of the project rather than the finished public surface.
For now it is public because the website prototype helps explain what Biblion is becoming and gives people a concrete artifact to explore.

sections.heading: What to open first
paragraphs: Start with the website entry document at [docs/website/index.html](../../../website/index.html). That page is the outer shell that loads the React runtime.
The behavior behind that page lives in [docs/website/src/App.jsx](../../../website/src/App.jsx), where the project overview graph, principles graph, and interaction flow are defined.

sections.heading: Why it matters
paragraphs: The prototype turns Biblion from a code repository into a navigable explanation of vision, architecture, development, and publication goals.
That matters for Patreon because ongoing support is easier to justify when supporters can see the shape of the platform and not just hear about it abstractly.

title: How the New Workflow Milestone Surface Changes Daily Work
excerpt: Explain the shared milestone model and why persistent project status matters across modules.
call_to_action: Members will get the detailed screenshots and iteration notes.

sections.heading: Angle
paragraphs: Show how the shared status bar and milestone dialog turn project progress into something visible and editable.
Use this to connect interface polish with actual scholarly workflow control.

title: From Scan to OCR: The Pipeline Biblion Is Assembling
excerpt: A public explainer about how acquisition, preprocessing, OCR, and post-processing fit together.
call_to_action: This is a good shareable overview for people following the project from outside the codebase.

sections.heading: Angle
paragraphs: Keep this approachable and narrative rather than deeply technical.
Emphasize why modularity helps preservation-grade workflows.

title: Why Infrastructure Housekeeping Counts as Real Progress
excerpt: A short post connecting Dependabot, docs, and publication hygiene to long-term project stability.
call_to_action: Use this to normalize maintenance work as part of serious software stewardship.

sections.heading: Angle
paragraphs: Translate invisible maintenance into visible trust-building.
Frame it as the cost of making a project durable enough for public scholarship.

title: Preservation Before Convenience: A Biblion Design Principle
excerpt: A principle-focused post that turns an internal design value into a public-facing argument.
call_to_action: This is a strong weekend post because it explains the philosophy behind many later technical decisions.

sections.heading: Angle
paragraphs: Connect source fidelity, traceability, and archival responsibility.
Avoid product-language and keep the emphasis on stewardship.

title: Lab Note: Making Scholarly Workflow Visible in the UI
excerpt: A more intimate members post about interface decisions, friction, and iteration logic.
call_to_action: Good slot for a candid post with rough edges and open questions.

sections.heading: Angle
paragraphs: Talk plainly about tradeoffs, status-bar density, and why editability matters more than polish at this stage.
This should feel closer to a studio journal than a polished announcement.

title: Week Ahead: What Support Enables Next
excerpt: A forward-looking wrap-up that sets the next week’s expectations and invites support.
call_to_action: Use this as the bridge into the next seven-post refresh.

sections.heading: Angle
paragraphs: Summarize what moved this week and what the next build targets are.
Keep the call for support concrete by naming the kind of work it makes possible.
